Entity_Description: Salmon Arm is a city in the Shuswap region of British Columbia, Canada, within the Columbia-Shuswap Regional District. As of the 2021 Census it had a population of 19,432. Set on Salmon Arm Bay at the south end of Shuswap Lake, the community straddles the Trans-Canada Highway roughly midway between Kamloops and Revelstoke. Its economy blends agriculture, forestry, and lake tourism, and it is known for the Salmon Arm Wharf, a western grebe nesting colony, the annual Roots and Blues Festival, and R.J. Haney Heritage Village.

Short_Description: Every spring, western grebes gather to nest along the Salmon Arm Wharf, one of the longest wooden wharves in North America and the emblem of a city that grew up where Shuswap Lake meets the Trans-Canada Highway. Salmon Arm anchors the Shuswap: a working blend of dairy farms, orchards, forestry, and lake tourism spread from downtown out to Canoe, Gleneden, and Silver Creek. That mix of waterfront property, rural acreage, and growing subdivisions is exactly why {BUSINESS_TYPE} work here demands local knowledge. {COMPANY_NAME} delivers {SERVICE} built for Salmon Arm's real conditions — not a one-size-fits-all script. Book your {ESTIMATE_TYPE} today by calling {PHONE}.
Long_Description:
Salmon Arm grew up around the sheltered bay that gives it its name, where the Salmon River empties into Shuswap Lake and the Trans-Canada Highway threads past the waterfront. Downtown, the shops along Hudson Avenue sit a short walk from Marine Peace Park and the Salmon Arm Wharf, while the surrounding neighbourhoods each carry their own character: lakefront Canoe to the northeast with its beach and old sawmill roots, orchard-dotted Gleneden to the west, and the rural acreages of Silver Creek climbing toward Mount Ida. {COMPANY_NAME} works across all of them, because a {SERVICE} plan for a Canoe waterfront home is not the plan for a hillside property off the Broadview bench.

The Shuswap climate shapes that work. Summers here are warm and increasingly smoky, but Salmon Arm is greener and wetter than the south Okanagan, with real snow loads through winter and a freeze-thaw shoulder season that tests any exterior. Add the humidity coming off Shuswap Lake and you get conditions that punish shortcuts — which is why {COMPANY_NAME} builds every {SERVICE} around what actually happens on a Salmon Arm property through the year, not a generic checklist.

The building stock is just as varied. You have heritage homes near the original townsite, postwar bungalows on the flats, view homes stepping up the hillsides, hobby farms and dairy operations out in Silver Creek, and a steady wave of newer subdivisions built as retirees and remote workers discover the Shuswap. That range is the whole reason local knowledge matters: {COMPANY_NAME} has learned how {PROPERTY_TYPE} properties across Salmon Arm differ, from a lakeshore cabin to a farm outbuilding to a new build near Little Mountain.

It is a community that takes pride in place — in the Roots and Blues Festival every summer, the trails at Larch Hills, and the heritage kept alive at R.J. Haney Heritage Village. {COMPANY_NAME} brings that same care to {SERVICE_CATEGORY}, combining {UNIQUE_VALUE_PROP} with genuine familiarity with the streets, seasons, and shorelines of Salmon Arm. Call {PHONE} to get started.
Local_Facts:
- Population: 19,432 (2021 Census)
- Region: Shuswap, within the Columbia-Shuswap Regional District
- Set on Salmon Arm Bay at the south end of Shuswap Lake
- The Trans-Canada Highway (Highway 1) runs directly through the city
- The Salmon Arm Wharf hosts a well-known nesting colony of western grebes
- Home to the annual Roots and Blues Festival and R.J. Haney Heritage Village
- Nearby communities include Armstrong and Spallumcheen (~28 km) and Vernon (~48 km)
